questions.prompt: Listen to CAP and CAB. Which sound changes between these two words?
questions.hint: The first two sounds match. Put your hand on your throat and say /p/ then /b/ — one makes your throat buzz.

questions.prompt: The word is TEN. If you change the FIRST sound to make HEN, which sound did you change?
questions.hint: Say TEN, then say HEN. The -en part stays exactly the same. Only listen to the very first sound.

questions.prompt: Listen to POT and PAT. Which sound is different in these two words?
questions.hint: The first sound /p/ and the last sound /t/ are the same in both. Stretch out the middle sound of each word.

standards.standard_description: Read emergent-reader texts with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Read simple books together and talk about what happened — 'what was the story about?'

questions.prompt:
I see a cat. The cat is big. The cat is on the mat.

Where is the CAT?
questions.hint: Read the last sentence — where is the cat sitting?

questions.prompt:
I like dogs. I like cats. I like fish. I like all pets!

What is this STORY about?
questions.hint: Dogs, cats, and fish are all types of...

questions.prompt:
I can run. I can hop. I can skip. I can do a lot!

What CAN'T this person do according to the story?
questions.hint: Read the sentences again. Which action is NOT in the story?

questions.prompt:
Mom said, "Get your coat." Dad said, "Get your hat." It is cold outside!

WHY did Mom and Dad tell the child to get a coat and hat?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you why!

questions.prompt:
Read it: Sam has a red hat. Pam has a red bag. The hat is on the bed.

Who has a red BAG?
questions.hint: Two kids each have a red thing. Read slowly and match the person to the RIGHT object — one has the hat, one has the bag.

questions.prompt:
Read it: The pig is not big. The pig is not sad. The pig is in the mud.

Where is the PIG?
questions.hint: The word 'not' changes the meaning. Find the sentence that tells WHERE the pig is, not what it is or isn't.

questions.prompt:
Read it: I can hop. I can run. I can not fly. Bugs can fly.

What can I NOT do?
questions.hint: Watch for the words 'can not'. Find the one thing that comes AFTER 'not', and remember bugs are the ones who fly.

questions.prompt:
Read it: The sun is up. It is hot. We sit in the shade. The shade is cool.

Why do we SIT in the shade?
questions.hint: The text says the sun is hot but the shade is a different word. Find the word that tells how the SHADE feels.

questions.prompt:
Read it: Deb fed the hen. The hen sat on an egg. Then the egg let out a chick.

What came out of the EGG?
questions.hint: The hen sat on the egg, but something ELSE came out of it. Read the last sentence and decode the word after 'a'.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking (nouns, verbs, plurals, question words, prepositions, complete sentences)
standards.parent_tip: Practice complete sentences at dinner — 'tell me about your day using a full sentence!'

questions.prompt: A NOUN is a person, place, or thing. Which word is a noun?
questions.hint: Can you touch it or point to it? That's a noun!

questions.prompt: Which word in the sentence 'The fish swims in the water' tells what the fish does?
questions.hint: Look for the word that shows the action the fish is doing.

questions.prompt: What is the PLURAL (more than one) of "cat"?
questions.hint: To make most words plural, just add an 's' at the end!

questions.prompt: Which word do you use to ask about a PLACE?
questions.hint: "_____ is the park?" — you're asking about a place!

questions.prompt: Finish the sentence with the correct word: 'There is one mouse by the wall, but I see three ___ by the cheese.'
questions.hint: Some words change in a special way when they mean more than one — they don't just add -s. Say each choice out loud and pick the one that sounds right for grown-up English.

questions.prompt: Which question word best fills the blank? '___ do you get to school?' The answer is: 'I ride the bus.'
questions.hint: Match the question word to the answer. 'I ride the bus' tells the WAY you travel, not a place, a person, or a time.

questions.prompt: Which word best fills the blank? 'The squirrel climbed ___ the tree to reach the nuts at the very top.'
questions.hint: A preposition tells where or which direction. Use the clue 'at the very top' to figure out which way the squirrel must go.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: 'We watched a funny play at school.' In which sentence below does the word 'play' mean the SAME thing?
choices: Do you want to play with me?
Let's play a game of tag.
The children play outside at recess.
Our class put on a play for the parents.
questions.correct: Our class put on a play for the parents.
questions.hint: In the top sentence, 'play' is a THING you watch, not something you do. Find the choice where 'play' is also a thing, not an action.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written correctly?
questions.hint: The sentence is about something that already happened. Some verbs don't add -ed for the past — they change into a new word instead.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing
standards.parent_tip: When writing together, remind about capitals at the start and periods at the end.

questions.prompt: This is a TELLING sentence. Pick the right ending.
questions.hint: A telling sentence (statement) ends with a period!

questions.prompt: Look at the sentence: "Do you like apples" Which mark belongs at the very end?
questions.hint: Remember, a question asks for information. What mark do we use for questions?

questions.prompt: Look at this sentence: "My friend is named sam." Which word needs a capital letter?
questions.hint: Remember that a person's name always starts with a big letter.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "My dog Max ran to me." One word is a NAME, so it must start with a capital letter. Which word is the name?
questions.hint: A name tells you what ONE special person or pet is called, not just what kind of animal it is.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is written the correct way?
questions.hint: Check the word 'I' AND check that only the first word gets a capital, not the middle words.

questions.prompt: Ben jumps up and shouts, "We won the big game___" Which mark best shows that Ben is very excited?
questions.hint: Think about how Ben's voice sounds when he is shouting and excited, not calm.

questions.prompt: Say the word 'stop' slowly: /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/. Which spelling matches those sounds correctly?
questions.hint: Stretch each sound; the sound right after /s/ is a /t/, even though it can sound a little like a /d/.

questions.prompt: Read this: "the sun is hot." Something is not correct. What needs to be fixed?
choices: The first word needs a capital T
It needs a question mark at the end
questions.correct: The first word needs a capital T
questions.hint: Every sentence must START in a special way. Look very closely at the first letter.

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases based on kindergarten reading and content
standards.parent_tip: When they encounter new words, look for clues: 'un-happy — un means not, so unhappy means not happy!'

questions.prompt:
The boy JUMPED. He went up in the air.

The **-ed** at the end of "jumped" tells us...
questions.hint: The '-ed' ending tells you about WHEN something happened.

questions.prompt:
The UNHAPPY girl frowned.

What does **UN-** at the beginning of **unhappy** mean?
questions.hint: Think about words like 'unsafe' or 'unkind' — what does 'un-' add?

questions.prompt: "She has two dogs." The **-s** on "dogs" means...
questions.hint: Two dogs is more than one dog. The '-s' is doing that work.

questions.prompt:
He was HELPFUL at school.

The **-ful** in **helpful** means...
questions.hint: If something is 'colorful' it has lots of color. What does '-ful' usually mean?

questions.prompt:
The CARELESS boy spilled his milk.

The **-less** in **careless** means...
questions.hint: If a treat is 'sugarless,' it has no sugar. What does '-less' mean?

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"The **bat** flew out of the dark cave when the sun went down."

What does **bat** mean here?
choices: A stick you use to hit a ball
questions.hint: The word 'bat' can mean more than one thing. Use the other words in THIS sentence — 'flew,' 'cave,' 'wings' — to pick the meaning that fits.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"**Duck!** The ball is flying right at your head!"

What does **duck** mean here?
choices: To bend down low very fast
questions.correct: To bend down low very fast
questions.hint: 'Duck' can be an animal OR an action. Ask yourself: what would you DO if a ball flew at your head?

questions.prompt:
The rain poured all day. The puppy was not just wet — he was **soaked**.

What does **soaked** most likely mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says 'not just wet.' That tells you 'soaked' is even MORE than plain wet — think about how much.

questions.prompt:
You know that **UN-** means 'the opposite.'

Mom used a key to **unlock** the door.

What does **unlock** mean?
questions.hint: 'Lock' means to close tight. 'Un-' flips it to the opposite. So what happens to a locked door?

questions.prompt: Which word means the MOST cold — colder than all the others?
questions.hint: Line the words up from a little cold to the MOST cold. Which one is the very strongest?

standards.standard_description: With guidance and support from adults, explore word relationships and nuances in word meanings
standards.parent_tip: Play sorting games with objects and talk about opposites — 'big vs small, hot vs cold.'

questions.prompt: Apple, banana, and orange are alike. Which word is also like them?
questions.hint: Think about what kind of things an apple, a banana, and an orange are.

questions.prompt: What is the OPPOSITE of "hot"?
questions.hint: Opposites are as different as can be. If something isn't hot, it's...

questions.prompt: Which word means to walk in a PROUD, fancy way?
questions.hint: Think of a peacock walking proudly — that's strutting!

questions.prompt: Dog, cat, and fish all belong to which CATEGORY?
questions.hint: They are all living creatures — what group do they belong to?

questions.prompt: These four words all mean cold: cool, chilly, cold, and freezing. Which word means the MOST cold of all?
questions.hint: Line the words up from a little cold to SO cold your teeth chatter. Which one is the very end?

questions.prompt: Read this sentence: "The boy swung the bat and hit the ball." What does the word "bat" mean here?
choices: A wooden stick you hit a ball with
A furry animal that flies at night
To blink your eyes very fast
questions.correct: A wooden stick you hit a ball with
questions.hint: Use the other words in the sentence. He SWUNG it to hit a BALL. Which meaning fits that action?

questions.prompt: A puppy is a baby dog. Which word names a baby cat?
questions.hint: Say each one to yourself: whose baby is it? Match the baby to the CAT, not to a bird, cow, or bear.

questions.prompt: All of these words mean to eat: nibble, taste, chew, and gobble. Which word means to eat A LOT very fast, like you are super hungry?
questions.hint: Some of these mean eating just a tiny bit. Which one sounds like eating fast and big?

questions.prompt: Red, blue, and green are all colors. Which word below is also a color?
questions.hint: The other three words describe things too, but they tell about a feeling, a shape, or a sound. Which one names a color?

standards.standard_description: Use words and phrases acquired through conversations, reading and being read to, and responding to texts
standards.parent_tip: Encourage using new words from books in conversation — 'remember the word enormous? Use it today!'

questions.prompt:
You read a book about the ocean. Now someone asks: "What lives in the ocean?"

The BEST answer is...
questions.hint: Think about what you learned from the ocean book!

questions.prompt:
Your teacher says: "Line up in a row."

What does "in a row" mean?
choices: One behind the other in a line
questions.correct: One behind the other in a line
questions.hint: When things are 'in a row,' they make a straight line!

questions.prompt:
You read a book about bears that said bears HIBERNATE in winter.

What does HIBERNATE mean?
questions.hint: Bears go to sleep for the whole winter — that's hibernating!

questions.prompt:
Your friend says: "That puzzle is really TRICKY."

What does TRICKY mean?
questions.hint: If something is tricky, you really have to think to solve it!

questions.prompt: When the leaves turn red and yellow and gently fall to the ground, what is another word we can use for this time of year?
questions.hint: Think about the different names we use for the seasons when the weather changes and leaves fall.

questions.prompt: After playing in the rain all day, Sam was not just a little wet — he was as wet as a person could ever be. Which word tells that Sam was the MOST wet?
questions.hint: Some words mean a small amount and some mean a big amount. Which one means MORE than just wet?

questions.prompt: A dog can bark, and a tree can have bark. Read this sentence: "The rough bark on the tree kept its trunk safe." What does the word "bark" mean HERE?
choices: The outside cover of a tree
To yell in a loud voice
questions.correct: The outside cover of a tree
questions.hint: Look at the other words in the sentence, like "tree" and "trunk." Which meaning fits with those?

questions.prompt: The soup just came off the hot stove and was bubbling and steaming. Which word tells that something is the MOST hot?
questions.hint: These words go from cold to very, very hot. Which one is the strongest?

questions.prompt: The word "trunk" can mean more than one thing. Read this sentence: "The elephant lifted the peanut with its long trunk." What does "trunk" mean in THIS sentence?
choices: The main part of a tree
questions.hint: Think about who is doing the action. What part of an elephant could lift a peanut?

questions.prompt: Maya got the exact puppy she had always wished for. She was not just a little happy — she was as happy as she could ever be. Which word tells that Maya felt the MOST happy?
questions.hint: Some feeling words are small and some are big. Which one shows the biggest, strongest happy feeling?

standards.standard_description: Actively engage in group reading activities with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Read every day! Mix favorite books with new ones to keep reading exciting.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: It was group story time. Miss Kim read a book to the class. The best listeners kept their eyes on the pictures and stayed quiet. What is one way to show you are actively listening during group story time?
choices: Talking to your friend next to you.
Looking at the pictures in the book as the teacher reads.
Thinking about what you will do after school.
questions.correct: Looking at the pictures in the book as the teacher reads.
questions.hint: Think about what your body does when you are really paying attention to a story being read aloud with your class.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Sam read a story, but some parts got too tricky. He did not give up or quit. He raised his hand and asked his teacher for help. What should you do when a story gets tricky?
questions.hint: When something is hard, what's a smart thing to do?

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The class read a story together. Ben listened closely and understood it well. He raised his hand and asked a good question about what happened. What is one way you can show you understand a story during group reading time?
choices: Talk about a different book you read yesterday.
Ask a question about what happened in the story.
Look at the wall instead of the book.
Keep quiet and not say anything at all.
questions.correct: Ask a question about what happened in the story.
questions.hint: Think about what you do when you hear something in a story that makes you curious or want to share your thoughts.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Miss Ray read a story to the whole class. As she read, she asked questions about it. The engaged children raised their hands and answered them. How can you show you are actively engaged when your teacher is reading a story to the whole class?
choices: Talk to your friend about your lunch
Look at the clock on the wall
questions.hint: Think about what it means to be part of the story and show you understand when everyone is reading together.

questions.prompt: Our class read a story about a dog named Pip. In the story, Pip lost his red ball. Pip looked and looked, but he could not find it. His tail hung down low. How does Pip feel in the story?
questions.hint: The words do not say how Pip feels. Look for clues, like what happened to his ball and what his tail is doing.

questions.prompt: The teacher read us a story. In it, the sky filled with dark gray clouds. Right away, the children in the story ran inside to grab their coats. Why did the children run to grab their coats?
choices: They wanted to play a game
questions.hint: Think about what dark gray clouds usually mean, then think about why you would want a coat.

questions.prompt: During story time we read about Mia. Mia planted one tiny seed in the dirt. She gave it water every single day. Now a red flower grows where the seed was. What made the flower grow?
choices: Mia gave it water every day
questions.correct: Mia gave it water every day
questions.hint: Read all the sentences. Find what Mia did over and over before the flower came.

questions.prompt: In our book, Sam gave a big yawn. He put on his soft pajamas. Outside his window the sky was dark, and many stars were out. What time is it in the story?
questions.hint: You cannot see a clock. Use three clues together: the pajamas, the dark sky, and the stars.

questions.prompt: Our class read about a boy named Ben. A new kid sat down with no snack and looked sad. Ben walked over and gave the new kid half of his own snack. Then they both smiled. Why did Ben share his snack?
choices: The new kid had no snack
Ben did not like his snack
questions.correct: The new kid had no snack
questions.hint: Look at what you learned about the new kid right before Ben shared.

standards.standard_description: Actively engage in group reading activities with informational texts with purpose and understanding
standards.parent_tip: Pick fact books about what your child loves — animals, weather, trucks, food.

questions.prompt: If we read a fact book that showed us pictures of lions, tigers, and bears, what would be the main topic of that book?
questions.hint: Think about what lions, tigers, and bears all are. The main topic tells you what the whole book is mostly about.

questions.prompt: You are reading a fact book about bugs with your group. What is a good way to actively engage with the book?
choices: Tell your friend a secret joke.
Ask your teacher a question about a bug in the book.
Close your eyes and listen to music.
Draw a picture of a flower on your hand.
questions.correct: Ask your teacher a question about a bug in the book.
questions.hint: Think about how you show you are interested and learning when you are reading with your friends and teacher.

questions.prompt: Your teacher is reading a book to help everyone learn about different animals. What is the best way to show you are learning?
choices: Close your eyes and try to guess what happens next.
Talk to your friend about playing outside later.
Look at the pictures and listen carefully to the words about animals.
Draw a picture of your favorite toy while the teacher reads.
questions.correct: Look at the pictures and listen carefully to the words about animals.
questions.hint: Think about what you do with your eyes and ears when you want to learn from a book.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Our group read a fact book about the sea. It had interesting pictures of fish and whales. We talked about the pictures together and learned new things. When your group reads a fact book, what is a good way to learn new things together?
choices: Only look at the words, not the pictures.
Keep all your good ideas quiet inside your head.
Talk about the interesting pictures with your friends.
Quickly turn all the pages without stopping.
questions.correct: Talk about the interesting pictures with your friends.
questions.hint: Think about how talking with others can help everyone understand new facts from a book.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Bees live in a hive. The hive keeps bees safe. A hive has many little rooms. Bees build the hive from wax." What is this page MOSTLY about?
questions.hint: Which thing is talked about in almost every sentence?

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A turtle has a hard shell. When a big dog comes close, the turtle pulls its head inside. It stays there until the dog walks away." Why does the turtle pull its head inside?
choices: To stay safe from the dog
questions.correct: To stay safe from the dog
questions.hint: Think about what happens right before the turtle hides, and what happens after.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Owls sleep all day long. Owls wake up when it is night. Owls look for food when they are awake." When do owls look for food?
choices: In the middle of the day
questions.hint: First find when owls are awake, then use that to find when they look for food.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A camel can go many days with no water. A fish must stay in water all the time. Both a camel and a fish need food to live." How are a camel and a fish the SAME?
choices: They both must stay in water
They both live in the sea
They both go days with no water
questions.hint: Most sentences tell how they are different. Look for the one thing they share.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact book: "This book shows how a seed grows. First a seed is planted in the dirt. Next it gets water and sun. Then a tiny green plant pokes up." What would this book MOST likely tell you NEXT?
choices: How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
How to bake a loaf of bread
What the plant looked like as a seed
questions.correct: How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
questions.hint: The book goes in order. Think about what comes after a tiny plant pokes up.
